curated_content.name: UML Best Practices and Professional Guidelines

aggregation_vs_composition.title: Composition (Strong Ownership) vs Aggregation (Weak Ownership)
aggregation_vs_composition.description: When modeling 'has-a' relationships, choosing between Composition (filled diamond ◆) and Aggregation (hollow diamond ◇) depends on lifecycle ownership. Composition means parts cannot exist without container (strong ownership, cascade delete). Aggregation means parts can exist independently (weak ownership). This is one of the most confusing aspects of UML for beginners.
aggregation_vs_composition.rationale: Composition vs Aggregation represents fundamental difference in object lifecycle management and ownership semantics. UML 2.5.1 specification clearly defines these relationship types, but proper application requires understanding system semantics. Composition enforces stronger coupling and lifecycle dependency, while Aggregation allows flexibility. Incorrect choice leads to implementation errors and misunderstood designs. Most experienced modelers default to Composition when true ownership exists and Association for weaker relationships, using Aggregation sparingly.

when_applicable: Parts are integral to the whole (use Composition)
Parts can exist independently (use Aggregation or Association)
Need to clarify ownership semantics in design
when_not_applicable: Is-a relationships (use Generalization/Inheritance)
Temporary usage (use Dependency)
General relationships without ownership (use Association)

options.name: Composition (Filled Diamond ◆)
options.reason: Strong ownership - parts cannot exist without container, lifecycle tightly coupled

pros: Clear lifecycle ownership (container deletion cascades to parts)
Strong encapsulation (parts belong to one container only)
Enforces design intent (parts are integral to whole)
Direct code implementation (private collection, owner manages lifecycle)
cons: May over-constrain design if requirements change

context_requirements: Parts are integral to container's identity

options.name: Aggregation (Hollow Diamond ◇)
options.reason: Weak ownership - parts can exist independently, more flexible but less common

pros: Parts can belong to multiple containers
cons: Semantics often unclear (weaker than Composition, stronger than Association)
Implementation ambiguous (no direct language support in Java, C#, etc.)
Many modelers avoid it (prefer Association or Composition)
Can confuse readers (when to use vs Association?)

context_requirements: Parts can belong to multiple containers

options.name: Association (Solid Line ────)
options.reason: General relationship without ownership semantics - often better than Aggregation

pros: Can add multiplicity and role names for clarity
cons: May need additional documentation to clarify lifecycle
Less precise than Composition for containment

decision_factors: Parts lifecycle (dependent → Composition; independent → Aggregation or Association)
Ownership (exclusive → Composition; shared → Aggregation or Association)
Cascade delete (yes → Composition; no → Aggregation or Association)
Clarity (prefer Composition or Association; use Aggregation only when semantics are clear)
Community practice (many prefer Composition + Association, avoid Aggregation)
Default: Use Composition for strong ownership, Association for general relationships

class_vs_sequence.title: Class Diagram (Structure) vs Sequence Diagram (Behavior)
class_vs_sequence.description: Class Diagrams show static system structure (what system is made of) while Sequence Diagrams show dynamic behavior (what system does over time). Both are fundamental but serve different purposes. For comprehensive documentation, use Class Diagrams to define structure, then Sequence Diagrams to show how classes interact.
class_vs_sequence.rationale: Class and Sequence Diagrams are the two most commonly used UML diagrams in practice. Class Diagrams are essential for object-oriented design, defining data structures, and code generation. Sequence Diagrams are essential for understanding complex interactions, API design, and debugging scenarios. Most projects need both. Class Diagrams answer 'what is the structure?' while Sequence Diagrams answer 'how does it work?'. Attempting to use one when the other is more appropriate leads to unclear documentation.

when_applicable: Need both structural and behavioral views
when_not_applicable: Simple scripts without objects (neither may be needed)
High-level business process (use Activity or Use Case)

options.name: Class Diagram
options.reason: Shows static structure - classes, attributes, operations, relationships

cons: Doesn't show timing or message sequence
Can become complex with many classes

options.name: Sequence Diagram
options.reason: Shows dynamic interactions over time - messages between objects

pros: Shows how objects interact over time
cons: Doesn't show class structure or attributes
Can become complex with many messages
May need multiple diagrams for different scenarios

decision_factors: Structure question (what is it made of?) → Class Diagram
Behavior question (how does it work?) → Sequence Diagram
Data model design → Class Diagram
Interaction design → Sequence Diagram
Long-term documentation → Class Diagram (structure stable)
Scenario documentation → Sequence Diagram (behavior-specific)
Best practice: Use BOTH for comprehensive documentation

agile_vs_comprehensive.title: Agile UML (Just Enough) vs Comprehensive Modeling (Complete Documentation)
agile_vs_comprehensive.description: Agile UML advocates 'just enough modeling' - create diagrams to answer specific questions, travel light, iterate. Comprehensive modeling creates complete documentation for all aspects. Agile UML is faster and more practical for modern development. Comprehensive modeling provides thorough documentation but is slower and may become outdated.
agile_vs_comprehensive.rationale: Modern software development favors agile/iterative approaches over waterfall/comprehensive upfront design. Agile UML aligns with this by focusing on creating diagrams that solve immediate problems rather than complete documentation. Comprehensive modeling can lead to analysis paralysis, outdated documentation, and wasted effort on diagrams no one uses. However, some domains (aerospace, medical devices, defense) require comprehensive documentation for regulatory compliance. The key is matching approach to context.
agile_vs_comprehensive.source: UML Best Practices, Agile Manifesto, Industry Experience

when_applicable: All software development projects (default to Agile UML)
when_not_applicable: Regulatory compliance requiring complete documentation
Fixed-bid contracts with detailed specifications
Very stable, long-term systems

options.name: Agile UML (Just Enough Modeling)
options.reason: Create diagrams to answer specific questions, iterate, travel light

pros: Stays relevant (update diagrams only when needed)
Lower maintenance burden (fewer diagrams to update)
cons: Incomplete documentation (intentional, but may cause gaps)
May need to create diagrams later

context_requirements: Team values working software over comprehensive documentation

options.name: Comprehensive Modeling
options.reason: Complete documentation for all aspects - thorough but slower and higher maintenance

pros: Suitable for regulatory compliance (FDA, DO-178C, etc.)
cons: Analysis paralysis (too much planning, not enough building)
High maintenance burden (many diagrams to update)
May document things that change (wasted effort)

context_requirements: Regulatory compliance required (medical devices, aerospace, defense)

decision_factors: Development approach (Agile → Just Enough; Waterfall → Comprehensive)
Regulatory constraints (FDA, DO-178C → Comprehensive; none → Just Enough)
Contract type (Fixed-bid detailed spec → Comprehensive; Agile contract → Just Enough)
System stability (Evolving → Just Enough; Very stable → Comprehensive)
Time pressure (High → Just Enough; Low → Comprehensive tolerable)
Default for modern development → Agile UML (Just Enough Modeling)

commercial_vs_opensource.title: Commercial UML Tools vs Open Source vs Whiteboard/Sketching
commercial_vs_opensource.description: Commercial tools (Enterprise Architect, Visual Paradigm) offer full features, code generation, and collaboration but cost money. Open source tools (Eclipse Papyrus, StarUML, PlantUML) are free but may have limited features. Whiteboard/sketching is fastest for agile/collaborative modeling but lacks persistence. Choice depends on needs, budget, and team size.
commercial_vs_opensource.rationale: Tool selection significantly impacts modeling effectiveness and team productivity. Commercial tools provide comprehensive features, professional support, and advanced capabilities (code generation, round-trip engineering, team collaboration) but require budget. Open source tools offer core functionality for free and work well for smaller teams or basic needs. Whiteboard/sketching (or drawing tools like draw.io) is fastest for agile teams focused on communication over documentation. Most teams benefit from lightweight tools (draw.io, PlantUML) or whiteboard for agile modeling, reserving commercial tools for large enterprises or Model-Driven Development.

when_applicable: All teams need some way to create UML diagrams
Team size, budget, and requirements vary
when_not_applicable: No UML modeling needed (very simple projects)

options.name: Whiteboard / Lightweight Tools (draw.io, Lucidchart)
options.reason: Fast, collaborative, good for agile teams - recommended for most projects

pros: Low/no cost (whiteboard free; draw.io free; Lucidchart affordable)
Aligns with Agile UML (just enough modeling)
Version control friendly (draw.io exports text-based XML)
cons: Limited validation (may not catch notation errors)

context_requirements: NOT needing code generation or round-trip engineering

options.name: Open Source Tools (Eclipse Papyrus, StarUML, PlantUML)
options.reason: Free with more features than sketching tools - good for budget-conscious teams

cons: Steeper learning curve (more complex than drawing tools)
May have bugs or incomplete features
Less polished UI than commercial tools

context_requirements: Budget constraints (no money for commercial tools)
Need code generation or XMI export
Technical team comfortable with tool complexity

options.name: Commercial Tools (Enterprise Architect, Visual Paradigm, MagicDraw)
options.reason: Full-featured, professional support, but expensive - only for large teams or MDD

pros: Full features (code generation, reverse engineering, round-trip)

decision_factors: Team size (small → Whiteboard/draw.io; large → Commercial)
Budget (limited → Open Source/draw.io; available → Commercial)
Development approach (Agile → Whiteboard/draw.io; MDD → Commercial)
Code generation needed (yes → Open Source or Commercial; no → Whiteboard/draw.io)
Collaboration needs (co-located → Whiteboard; distributed → draw.io/Lucidchart/Commercial)
Default recommendation → draw.io or Whiteboard for most agile teams

mermaid_vs_traditional_tools.title: Prefer Mermaid for Documentation and Collaboration, Traditional Tools for Formal Specifications
mermaid_vs_traditional_tools.description: For developer-centric documentation, living documentation in repos, and collaborative environments, use Mermaid (text-based, version control friendly). For formal specifications, regulatory documentation, and complex enterprise modeling, use traditional UML tools (Visio, Lucidchart, Enterprise Architect).
mermaid_vs_traditional_tools.rationale: Mermaid's text-based approach enables diagrams as code, makes them version control friendly (git diff/blame works), embeds in README files and wikis, requires no special tools (renders in GitHub/GitLab), and enables developers to quickly create and update diagrams. Traditional tools offer more complete UML 2.5.1 compliance, code generation, detailed modeling features, but create binary files that don't diff well and require licensed software. The choice depends on audience (developers vs formal stakeholders), documentation type (living vs formal), and compliance requirements.

when_applicable: Living documentation that evolves with code
when_not_applicable: Regulatory compliance documentation (FDA, aerospace)
Formal enterprise architecture (TOGAF, Zachman)
Code generation from models required
Complex state machines with nested states
Very large models (100+ classes)
Stakeholders require traditional tool output

options.name: Mermaid (Text-Based)
options.reason: Ideal for developer collaboration, version control, living documentation

pros: Text-based (version control friendly, git diff works)
No special tools required (renders in Markdown, GitHub, GitLab, Confluence)
Free and open source (no licensing costs)
Living documentation (stays with code in repo)
Quick to create and update (just edit text)
Supports 14+ diagram types (Flowchart, Sequence, Class, ERD, State, etc.)
Renders in documentation platforms (ReadMe, Quarto, Notion)
cons: Limited styling options vs traditional tools
Less suitable for very complex models
No visual editor (text editing only, though mermaid.live helps)
Not all UML 2.5.1 features supported (e.g., Profile diagrams)

options.name: Traditional UML Tools (Visio, Lucidchart, Enterprise Architect, Visual Paradigm)
options.reason: Necessary for formal specifications, regulatory compliance, code generation

pros: Code generation from models (Java, C#, C++)
Export to multiple formats (PDF, PNG, XMI)
cons: Binary files (poor version control, git diff doesn't work)

decision_factors: Audience (developers → Mermaid; formal stakeholders → traditional tools)
Documentation type (living → Mermaid; formal/regulatory → traditional)
Version control priority (high → Mermaid; low → traditional)
Code generation needed (yes → traditional; no → Mermaid)
Budget (limited → Mermaid free; available → traditional)
Collaboration model (team editing → Mermaid; sequential → traditional)
Default recommendation → Mermaid for 80% of developer needs, traditional for regulatory/MDD

dfd_notation_style.title: Use Gane-Sarson DFD Notation for Industry Projects, Yourdon-DeMarco for Academic
dfd_notation_style.description: Gane-Sarson notation (rounded rectangles for processes, open rectangles for data stores) is the industry standard for DFDs and should be the default choice for professional projects. Yourdon-DeMarco notation (circles for processes) is more common in academic settings and older texts.
dfd_notation_style.rationale: Gane-Sarson notation is more compact, easier to read, and widely used in industry tools (Visio, Lucidchart, Enterprise Architect). Rounded rectangles allow room for process numbers and names. The notation is consistently applied across CASE tools and is what business analysts and system analysts expect to see. Yourdon-DeMarco, while historically significant, is less space-efficient and primarily seen in academic contexts.
dfd_notation_style.source: Sparx Systems DFD Guide, Industry Best Practices

when_not_applicable: Academic coursework following specific notation requirement
Maintaining existing DFDs in Yourdon-DeMarco notation
Historical documentation that must match old notation

options.name: Gane-Sarson Notation
options.reason: Industry standard, compact, tool-supported, easier to read

pros: Industry standard (expected by business analysts, system analysts)
Rounded rectangles easier to read and label
Supported by all major tools (Visio, Lucidchart, EA)
Process numbers and names fit well in rounded rectangles
Clear distinction between components (square vs rounded vs open rectangle)
cons: Less common in older academic textbooks
Not the original DFD notation (Yourdon-DeMarco came first)
May require re-learning if taught Yourdon-DeMarco

options.name: Yourdon-DeMarco Notation
options.reason: Academic use, historical significance, specific course requirements

pros: Common in academic textbooks and courses
cons: Less common in modern industry tools
Can look cluttered with complex diagrams
Not expected by most business stakeholders

decision_factors: Context (industry → Gane-Sarson; academic → may require Yourdon-DeMarco)
Tool support (modern tools → Gane-Sarson)
Stakeholder expectations (business analysts → Gane-Sarson)
Space efficiency (Gane-Sarson more compact)
Default recommendation → Gane-Sarson for 90% of projects

erd_notation_style.title: Use Crow's Foot ERD Notation for Database Design, Chen for Academic, UML for OOP
erd_notation_style.description: Crow's Foot notation is the industry standard for database design ERDs, providing compact representation with clear cardinality symbols. Chen notation is more common in academic settings. UML Class Diagrams are appropriate when combining database structure with object-oriented design (methods, inheritance).
erd_notation_style.rationale: Crow's Foot notation is universally used in industry database design tools (MySQL Workbench, SQL Server Management Studio, pgModeler, dbdiagram.io, Lucidchart). It's compact (attributes inside entity rectangles), cardinality symbols are intuitive (crow's foot = many, single line = one, circle = optional), and it's what DBAs and database developers expect. Chen notation is more verbose (attributes as separate ovals) and primarily seen in database theory courses. UML Class Diagrams are appropriate when modeling both database structure AND object-oriented behavior (methods, inheritance), but are overkill for pure database design.
erd_notation_style.source: Lucidchart ERD Guide, Database Design Best Practices

when_applicable: Communication with DBAs and database developers
when_not_applicable: Academic coursework requiring specific notation
Object-oriented design requiring methods and inheritance (use UML Class Diagram)
Maintaining existing ERDs in different notation

options.name: Crow's Foot Notation
options.reason: Industry standard for database design, compact, intuitive cardinality

pros: Industry standard (expected by DBAs, database developers)
Intuitive cardinality symbols (crow's foot = many, │ = one, ○ = zero)
Supported by all major database tools (MySQL Workbench, SSMS, pgModeler)
Clear visual representation (cardinality at both ends of relationship)
cons: Less detailed than Chen (no attribute shapes)
Symbols require learning (crow's foot, circle, bar)

options.name: Chen Notation
options.reason: Academic use, original ER model notation, detailed attribute representation

pros: Detailed attribute representation (ovals show attribute types)
Clear separation of entities, attributes, relationships (distinct shapes)
Shows composite, multi-valued, derived attributes explicitly
cons: Less common in modern industry tools
Can become cluttered with many attributes
Not expected by most database professionals

options.name: UML Class Diagram
options.reason: When combining database structure with object-oriented design

pros: Shows both data structure AND behavior (methods)
cons: Methods not relevant for pure database schema

context_requirements: ORM framework usage (Entity Framework, Hibernate, SQLAlchemy)
Need to model both database AND object behavior

decision_factors: Purpose (database design → Crow's Foot; OOP + database → UML; academic theory → Chen)
Audience (DBAs → Crow's Foot; developers with OOP → UML; students → Chen)
Tool support (database tools → Crow's Foot; UML tools → UML)
Complexity (simple database → Crow's Foot; database + behavior → UML)
Default recommendation → Crow's Foot for 85% of database design projects

document_template_selection.title: Use SOW for Contracts, SRS for Requirements, Technical Spec for Design
document_template_selection.description: Select document template based on project phase and purpose: Statement of Work (SOW) for contractual agreements defining scope, deliverables, and timeline; Software Requirements Specification (SRS) for capturing functional and non-functional requirements; Technical Specification (Design Spec) for describing implementation approach and architecture. Each serves different audiences and project phases.
document_template_selection.rationale: Document templates serve distinct purposes in the software development lifecycle. SOW is a legal/contractual document for client-vendor agreements, focusing on what will be delivered, when, and at what cost. SRS is a requirements document for developers, testers, and stakeholders, focusing on WHAT the system must do (functionality, performance, security). Technical Spec is a design document for developers and architects, focusing on HOW the system will be implemented (architecture, APIs, data models). Using the wrong template creates confusion, missing critical information, and communication failures. Following IEEE standards (830-1998, 1016-2009, 29148-2018) ensures professional, complete documentation.

when_not_applicable: Proof-of-concept prototypes (may skip formal docs)
Very small internal tools (README may suffice)
Agile projects with living documentation (may use lightweight alternatives)

options.name: Statement of Work (SOW)
options.reason: Essential for contracts, defines scope, deliverables, timeline, costs

pros: Legal contract between client and vendor
cons: Formal change management needed for modifications
May feel bureaucratic for small projects

options.name: Software Requirements Specification (SRS)
options.reason: Captures all requirements (functional and non-functional), basis for development and testing

pros: Basis for development (developers know WHAT to build)
Basis for testing (testers know WHAT to verify)
cons: Can become lengthy (100+ pages for complex systems)
May feel heavy for Agile teams (prefer user stories)
Risk of becoming outdated if not maintained

options.name: Technical Specification (Design Spec)
options.reason: Describes implementation approach, architecture, design decisions

pros: Basis for code reviews (does code match design?)
cons: Can become outdated as code evolves
May feel like 'Big Design Up Front' (BDUF) for Agile teams
Requires architectural skill to write well
Risk of over-design if too detailed

decision_factors: Project phase (contract → SOW; requirements → SRS; design → Technical Spec)
Audience (legal/client → SOW; developers/testers/BA → SRS; architects/developers → Technical Spec)
Purpose (scope agreement → SOW; WHAT system does → SRS; HOW system built → Technical Spec)
Regulatory (FDA/FAA/automotive → all three mandatory; internal projects → may skip SOW)
Project size (small internal tool → may use README only; enterprise → all three)
Default recommendation → SRS and Technical Spec for most professional projects, SOW for contracts

ascii_art_absolutely_forbidden.title: ASCII Art Diagrams Are STRICTLY FORBIDDEN - Use Mermaid or SVG ONLY
ascii_art_absolutely_forbidden.description: ASCII art diagrams (boxes made of characters, text-based arrows) are absolutely forbidden in all professional documentation. They are unprofessional, unreadable, break rendering, and produce terrible quality output. MANDATORY: Use Mermaid for text-based diagrams that render properly, or SVG when Mermaid has limitations. There is NO acceptable use case for ASCII art in professional documentation.
ascii_art_absolutely_forbidden.rationale: ASCII art diagrams fail on every quality metric: (1) Unprofessional appearance - looks like code from the 1980s; (2) Poor readability - breaks on different fonts, terminals, editors; (3) Inconsistent rendering - looks different in every viewer; (4) Not maintainable - difficult to edit, align, update; (5) Accessibility issues - screen readers cannot interpret; (6) No styling options - monochrome, fixed size; (7) Breaks in proportional fonts - designed for monospace only. Mermaid provides text-based diagrams that render as professional graphics in all modern documentation platforms (GitHub, GitLab, Confluence, Notion, wikis). SVG provides professional vector graphics when Mermaid limitations are encountered. There is absolutely no justification for using ASCII art in professional documentation - it is a quality and professionalism failure.

when_applicable: This rule applies universally with ZERO exceptions
when_not_applicable: NEVER - There is no situation where ASCII art is acceptable
Not even for 'quick sketches' - use Mermaid instead
Not even for 'simple diagrams' - Mermaid is just as easy
Not even for 'placeholder diagrams' - start with Mermaid from day one

options.name: Mermaid Diagrams (Text-Based, Renders Properly)
options.reason: Professional rendering, version control friendly, universal platform support

pros: Text-based source (version control friendly, git diff works)
Universal support (GitHub, GitLab, Confluence, Notion, wikis)
Scales properly (not fixed to monospace grid)
Easy to write (simpler syntax than ASCII art alignment)
cons: Requires basic syntax knowledge (5 minutes to learn)
May have limitations for very complex custom layouts (use SVG instead)

context_requirements: Flowcharts, Sequence, Class, ERD, State, DFD, etc.
This is the default and mandatory choice

options.name: SVG (Scalable Vector Graphics)
options.reason: Professional vector graphics for complex diagrams Mermaid cannot handle

pros: Can be created with draw.io, Lucidchart, Inkscape, Adobe Illustrator
cons: Larger file size than Mermaid source
Git diffs less readable than Mermaid text
Not as quick to create as Mermaid

context_requirements: When Mermaid limitations are definitively encountered

options.name: ASCII Art (Character-Based Diagrams)
options.reason: ABSOLUTELY FORBIDDEN - Unprofessional, unreadable, terrible quality

pros: NONE - There are no advantages to ASCII art
Any perceived advantage (text-based, no tools) is better served by Mermaid
cons: Unreadable (breaks on different fonts, terminals, proportional fonts)
Not maintainable (nightmare to align, edit, update)
No styling options (monochrome, fixed size, ugly)
Breaks in modern editors with proportional fonts

context_requirements: If you're considering ASCII art, use Mermaid instead
Violation of this rule is a critical documentation failure

decision_factors: Can Mermaid represent this diagram? (YES → Use Mermaid, NO → Use SVG)
Is this ASCII art? (YES → DELETE IT AND USE MERMAID OR SVG)
Default for 95% of diagrams → Mermaid
For complex custom layouts → SVG
NEVER EVER under any circumstances → ASCII art (forbidden)
